%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# B.2 Addition of Sinusoids

> **Section Objective:** Learn how to combine two same-frequency sinusoids into one sinusoid using phasors.

---

## Concepts In This Section

- Same-frequency sinusoid addition
- Phasor representation
- Amplitude and phase from \(a - jb\)
- Quadrant-safe angle calculation
- Representative worked example

## 1. The core formula: one sinusoid replaces two

$$C\sin(\omega_0 t+\pi/2)=C\cos(\omega_0 t)\[4pt]a\cos\omega_0t+b\sin\omega_0t=C\cos(\omega_0t+\theta)\quad\text{where}\quad a=C\cos\theta,\; b=-C\sin\theta\[4pt]C=\sqrt{a^2+b^2},\qquad \theta=\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{-b}{a}\right)\;\text{with correct quadrant}\[4pt]a-jb=Ce^{j\theta}$$
## 1. The core formula: one sinusoid replaces two

This substitution works **only** when both terms share the same angular frequency \(\omega_0\). If the frequencies differ, the formula does not apply.

The term \(a\cos(\omega_0 t)\) contributes a **horizontal** phasor component of length \(a\). The term \(b\sin(\omega_0 t)\) equals \(b\cos(\omega_0 t - \pi/2)\), which contributes a **vertical** phasor component. Together they define the complex number \(a - jb\).

**Symbol guide:**
- \(a\), \(b\) — real coefficients of the cosine and sine terms
- \(C = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}\) — resulting amplitude (phasor length)
- \(\theta\) — phase shift (phasor angle)
- \(\omega_0\) — angular frequency, unchanged by this operation
- \(j\) — marks the imaginary-axis direction

**Exam trigger phrase:** "express as a single sinusoid."

**Common misuse:** applying \(\tan^{-1}(-b/a)\) without checking which quadrant \(a - jb\) actually occupies.

**Minimal example:** For \(a = 1\) and \(b = -\sqrt{3}\), the phasor is \(1 - j(-\sqrt{3}) = 1 + j\sqrt{3}\), so \(C = 2\) and \(\theta = 60°\).


## 2. See the phasor addition happen

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

## 3. Representative example: combine cos minus sqrt(3) sin

Given:
$$
x(t) = \cos(\omega_0 t) - \sqrt{3}\sin(\omega_0 t)
$$

**Step 1 — Identify coefficients.**
\(a = 1\), \(b = -\sqrt{3}\).

**Step 2 — Compute amplitude.**
$$
C = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2} = \sqrt{1^2 + (-\sqrt{3})^2} = \sqrt{1 + 3} = 2
$$

**Step 3 — Form the phasor \(a - jb\).**
$$
a - jb = 1 - j(-\sqrt{3}) = 1 + j\sqrt{3}
$$
Because \(b\) is negative, \(-jb\) becomes **positive imaginary**, so the phasor points **above** the real axis — into the first quadrant.

**Step 4 — Read the angle.**
$$
\theta = \angle(1 + j\sqrt{3}) = 60°
$$

**Result:**
$$
x(t) = 2\cos(\omega_0 t + 60°)
$$

> **Exam note:** The angular frequency \(\omega_0\) is unchanged. Only the amplitude and phase are affected by this operation. Never alter \(\omega_0\) when combining same-frequency sinusoids.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="trap_exposure" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 The left plane shows why using \(\tan^{-1}(-b/a)\) alone is dangerous — the same ratio can point to two different quadrants. The right plane shows the correct approach: plot \(a - jb = -3 - j4\) first, confirm it lies in quadrant III, then read \(\theta = -126.9°\).*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1777969895820-2406.png)

## 4. Exam trap: the arctangent angle must match the phasor

Consider Example B.6(b):
$$
x(t) = -3\cos(\omega_0 t) + 4\sin(\omega_0 t)
$$

**Identify coefficients:** \(a = -3\), \(b = 4\).

**Form the phasor:**
$$
a - jb = -3 - j4
$$
This point lies in **quadrant III** of the complex plane (both real and imaginary parts are negative).

**Compute amplitude:**
$$
C = \sqrt{(-3)^2 + (-4)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = 5
$$

**Compute angle correctly:**
$$
\theta = \angle(-3 - j4) = -126.9°
$$

**Result:**
$$
x(t) = 5\cos(\omega_0 t - 126.9°)
$$

### COMMON MISTAKE

A calculator returns \(\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{-4}{-3}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1.33) \approx 53.1°\). This is the **wrong quadrant** — \(53.1°\) points into quadrant I, but the phasor \(-3 - j4\) is in quadrant III.

> **Quick check:** Before finalizing \(\theta\), always ask: does my angle point to the same quadrant as \(a - jb\)?

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Same frequency \(\omega_0\) is unchanged — only amplitude and phase are affected.
- \(C = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}\) is the resultant phasor length.
- \(\theta\) is the angle of the complex number \(a - jb\), not just \(\tan^{-1}(-b/a)\).
- Always verify that \(\theta\) lies in the same quadrant as \(a - jb\) before finalizing.

*In the next section we will use this phasor viewpoint to simplify more sinusoidal calculations.*
